chapters.title: Food: Where Does It Come From?

concepts.title: Food and Its Sources
concepts.definition: Food is any nutritious substance that living organisms eat or drink to maintain life and growth. Food materials come mainly from plants and animals.
examples: We get rice, wheat, fruits and vegetables from plants.
We get milk, eggs, meat and honey from animals.

concepts.title: Plant Parts as Food
concepts.definition: Different parts of plants such as roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits and seeds are used as food.
examples: Carrot and radish are edible roots; potato is an underground stem; spinach is a leaf; cauliflower is a flower; apple is a fruit; wheat and gram are seeds.

concepts.title: Animal Products as Food
concepts.definition: Animals provide many food items such as milk, meat, eggs, honey and fish.
examples: Cows and buffaloes give milk; hens give eggs; bees make honey from nectar of flowers.

concepts.title: Herbivores, Carnivores and Omnivores
concepts.definition: Animals are grouped by their food habits: herbivores eat only plants, carnivores eat only other animals and omnivores eat both plants and animals.
examples: Cow and goat are herbivores; lion and tiger are carnivores; humans and bears are omnivores.

concepts.title: Nutrients and Their Types
concepts.definition: Nutrients are substances in food that our body needs for energy, growth, repair and maintenance. Major nutrients are carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, along with water and roughage.
examples: Rice and sugar are rich in carbohydrates; pulses and meat in proteins; oils and ghee in fats; fruits and vegetables in vitamins and minerals.

concepts.title: Functions of Nutrients
concepts.definition: Carbohydrates and fats mainly give energy; proteins help in growth and repair; vitamins and minerals protect us from diseases and keep the body working properly; water and roughage help digestion and removal of wastes.
examples: Growing children need more protein to build body tissues.
Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy; iron deficiency causes anaemia.

concepts.title: Balanced Diet
concepts.definition: A balanced diet contains all nutrients, water and roughage in proper amounts needed by the body for good health.
examples: A plate with chapati or rice, dal, vegetables, some salad and curd is closer to a balanced diet than only chips or sweets.

concepts.title: Deficiency Diseases
concepts.definition: Lack of one or more nutrients over a long period leads to deficiency diseases with specific symptoms.
examples: Lack of vitamin D causes rickets (weak, bent bones); lack of iodine causes goitre (swollen neck).

concepts.title: Fibres, Yarn and Fabric
concepts.definition: Fibres are thin strands that can be spun into yarn; yarn is a long continuous thread; fabric is cloth made by weaving or knitting yarn.
examples: Cotton fibres are spun into cotton yarn which is woven into cloth.
Wool yarn is knitted into sweaters.

concepts.title: Plant Fibres: Cotton and Jute
concepts.definition: Cotton comes from cotton bolls on cotton plants; jute comes from the stem of jute plants.
examples: In cotton ginning, cotton fibres are separated from seeds.
Jute stems are soaked in water and then fibres are stripped off.

concepts.title: Spinning and Weaving
concepts.definition: Spinning converts fibres into yarn by twisting; weaving makes fabric by crossing two sets of yarns at right angles on a loom.
examples: Charkha and spinning wheels are used for spinning; handlooms and powerlooms are used for weaving.

concepts.title: Objects and Materials
concepts.definition: Objects are things we see and use; they are made from different materials such as wood, metal, glass, plastic and cotton.
examples: A chair can be made of wood or plastic; utensils are often made of steel or aluminium.

concepts.title: Properties of Materials
concepts.definition: Materials are grouped based on properties such as appearance, hardness, solubility, transparency, conduction of heat and electricity.
examples: Metals are shiny and hard; chalk is dull and soft; salt dissolves in water but sand does not.

concepts.title: Transparent, Translucent and Opaque
concepts.definition: Transparent materials allow almost all light to pass; translucent allow some light; opaque do not allow light to pass.
examples: Clear glass is transparent; oiled paper is translucent; wood and metal are opaque.

concepts.title: Need for Separation
concepts.definition: Substances are separated to remove impurities, get useful components, or obtain pure samples.
examples: Stones are removed from rice; cream is separated from milk; salt is separated from sand.

concepts.title: Methods of Separation
concepts.definition: Different methods like handpicking, threshing, winnowing, sieving, filtration, evaporation and decantation are used depending on properties of components.
examples: Winnowing separates husk from grain using wind; sieving separates flour from coarser particles.

concepts.title: Soluble and Insoluble Substances
concepts.definition: Substances that dissolve in a given liquid form solutions; those that do not dissolve are insoluble and can often be separated by filtration.
examples: Salt is soluble in water; sand is insoluble and can be removed by filtration.

concepts.title: Evaporation and Condensation in Separation
concepts.definition: Evaporation removes the liquid part to obtain dissolved solids; condensation converts vapour back to liquid.
examples: Salt is obtained from seawater by evaporation; distillation uses evaporation followed by condensation to purify liquids.

concepts.title: Different Types of Changes
concepts.definition: Changes occur when things are heated, cooled, mixed, stretched or compressed; they can affect shape, size, state or colour.
examples: Melting wax, stretching a rubber band, mixing sugar in water are all changes.

concepts.title: Reversible and Irreversible Changes
concepts.definition: Reversible changes can be undone to get the original material back; irreversible changes cannot be reversed easily.
examples: Melting of ice is reversible; burning of paper is irreversible.

concepts.title: Physical and Chemical Changes (Intro)
concepts.definition: Physical changes only alter physical properties and do not form new substances; chemical changes form new substances with new properties.
examples: Folding paper is a physical change; formation of curd from milk is a chemical change.

concepts.title: Herbs, Shrubs and Trees
concepts.definition: Plants are grouped by size and stem type: herbs are small with soft stems, shrubs are medium‑sized with woody stems branching from base, trees are tall with a single woody stem.
examples: Mint is a herb; rose is a shrub; neem and mango are trees.

concepts.title: Parts of Plants
concepts.definition: Main parts are root, stem, leaf, flower and fruit; each part has specific roles in support, transport, photosynthesis and reproduction.
examples: Roots anchor the plant and absorb water; leaves make food; flowers form fruits and seeds.

concepts.title: Types of Roots and Leaves
concepts.definition: Roots may be tap roots or fibrous roots. Leaves have veins and midrib; they show venation patterns like reticulate or parallel.
examples: Pea has tap root; wheat has fibrous roots. Mango leaf shows reticulate venation; grass leaf shows parallel venation.

concepts.title: Functions of Leaves and Photosynthesis (Intro)
concepts.definition: Leaves prepare food using sunlight, carbon dioxide and water in a process called photosynthesis, often indicated by presence of chlorophyll and stomata.
examples: Boiled leaf iodine test shows presence of starch, evidence of photosynthesis.

concepts.title: Movement and Locomotion
concepts.definition: Movement is change in position of a body part; locomotion is movement that takes the whole body from one place to another.
examples: Bending an arm is movement; walking from one room to another is locomotion.

concepts.title: Human Skeletal System
concepts.definition: The skeleton is the framework of bones and cartilage that gives shape, protects organs and helps in movement with the help of muscles.
examples: The skull protects the brain; rib cage protects heart and lungs.

concepts.title: Joints and Their Types
concepts.definition: Joints are places where two or more bones meet; types include ball‑and‑socket, hinge, pivot and fixed joints.
examples: Shoulder and hip have ball‑and‑socket joints; elbow and knee have hinge joints; neck has a pivot joint.

concepts.title: Movement in Different Animals
concepts.definition: Animals have different body structures and move in various ways such as walking, running, crawling, flying and swimming.
examples: Earthworm moves by contracting and expanding its body; fish uses fins and tail to swim; birds use wings to fly.

chapters.title: The Living Organisms and Their Surroundings

concepts.title: Habitat and Adaptation
concepts.definition: Habitat is the natural home or environment of an organism. Adaptations are special features that help organisms survive in their habitat.
examples: Fish live in water habitat with gills and fins; camels live in deserts with humps and long legs.

concepts.title: Types of Habitats
concepts.definition: Main habitat types include terrestrial (land) and aquatic (water), further divided into deserts, forests, mountains, freshwater and marine habitats.
examples: Cactus grows in desert habitat; pine trees grow in mountain habitat.

concepts.title: Characteristics of Living Organisms
concepts.definition: Living organisms show characteristics such as growth, movement, response to stimuli, need for food, respiration, excretion and reproduction.
examples: Plants bend towards light (response to stimulus); humans grow from babies to adults.

concepts.title: Need for Standard Units
concepts.definition: Standard units of measurement ensure that everyone measures quantities like length in the same way everywhere.
examples: Earlier non‑standard units like handspan or foot varied from person to person; metre is a standard unit of length.

concepts.title: Units of Length and Conversion
concepts.definition: Standard unit of length in SI is metre; smaller units include centimetre and millimetre; kilometre is used for large distances.
examples: A pencil may be 15 cm long; distance between cities measured in kilometres.

concepts.title: Measuring Length
concepts.definition: Length is measured using rulers, measuring tapes, metre rods etc., keeping zero mark properly aligned and eye in correct position to avoid errors.
examples: To measure a table, place the metre scale along its edge with zero at one end.

concepts.title: Types of Motion
concepts.definition: Motion can be rectilinear (straight line), circular, periodic or random depending on path and pattern.
examples: A car on a straight road shows rectilinear motion; a child on a swing shows periodic motion; the tip of a clock’s second hand shows circular motion.

concepts.title: Luminous and Non-Luminous Objects
concepts.definition: Luminous objects produce their own light; non‑luminous objects do not and are seen by reflected light.
examples: The Sun and an electric bulb are luminous; a chair and book are non‑luminous.

concepts.title: Shadow Formation
concepts.definition: A shadow is formed on a screen when an opaque object blocks light from a source; it needs a light source, an object and a screen.
examples: Your hand blocking torchlight makes a shadow on the wall.

concepts.title: Transparent, Translucent and Opaque (Light Context)
concepts.definition: Transparent objects let most light pass and form faint shadows; translucent let some light through and form blurred shadows; opaque form dark, clear shadows.
examples: Thin cloth is translucent; thick cardboard is opaque.

concepts.title: Reflection in Plane Mirrors (Intro)
concepts.definition: Reflection is bouncing back of light from a surface; a plane mirror reflects light and forms an image that looks like the object but reversed left to right.
examples: Your right hand appears as left in a mirror due to lateral inversion.

concepts.title: Electric Cell and Battery
concepts.definition: An electric cell is a small source of electrical energy with two terminals, positive and negative; a battery is a group of cells connected together.
examples: Torch cells connected in series form a battery providing more voltage.

concepts.title: Electric Circuit
concepts.definition: An electric circuit is a closed path along which current can flow from the cell through wires and devices back to the cell.
examples: A simple circuit includes a cell, switch, bulb and connecting wires.

concepts.title: Conductors and Insulators
concepts.definition: Conductors allow electric current to pass through easily; insulators do not.
examples: Metals like copper and aluminium are conductors; rubber, plastic and wood are insulators.

concepts.title: Bulb and Its Glowing
concepts.definition: A bulb has a thin filament that glows when electric current passes through, converting electrical energy to light and heat.
examples: If the filament breaks, the circuit is open and the bulb does not glow.

concepts.title: Discovery and Types of Magnets
concepts.definition: Magnets attract certain metals like iron and were first discovered as natural magnets called lodestones; magnets can be bar, horseshoe or ring shaped.
examples: A bar magnet has two ends called poles; a horseshoe magnet is shaped like a U.

concepts.title: Magnetic and Non-Magnetic Materials
concepts.definition: Magnetic materials are attracted by a magnet; non‑magnetic materials are not.
examples: Iron, nickel and cobalt are magnetic; wood, plastic and glass are non‑magnetic.

concepts.title: Magnetic Poles and Their Properties
concepts.definition: Magnets have two poles, north and south; like poles repel each other and unlike poles attract each other.
examples: Two north poles put together repel; a north and south pole attract.

concepts.title: Making and Caring for Magnets
concepts.definition: Magnets can be made by stroking with a magnet or by electricity and should be stored properly to retain magnetism.
examples: Bar magnets are stored in pairs with keepers (soft iron pieces) across poles to reduce loss of magnetism.

concepts.title: Sources and Uses of Water
concepts.definition: Water is obtained from sources like rain, rivers, lakes, ponds, wells and oceans and is essential for drinking, agriculture, industry and domestic use.
examples: Rainwater fills rivers and lakes which supply water to cities and villages.

concepts.title: Water Cycle
concepts.definition: The water cycle is continuous movement of water between land, water bodies and atmosphere through evaporation, condensation and precipitation.
examples: Water from seas evaporates, forms clouds and returns as rain, snow or hail.

concepts.title: States of Water and Change of State
concepts.definition: Water exists as solid (ice), liquid (water) and gas (water vapour) and can change state by heating or cooling.
examples: Ice melts to water when heated; water boils to steam; steam condenses to water on cooling.

concepts.title: Need to Conserve Water
concepts.definition: Freshwater is limited and must be conserved by preventing wastage and pollution and by reusing and storing water.
examples: Closing taps properly, repairing leaks and using rainwater harvesting help save water.

concepts.title: Composition of Air
concepts.definition: Air is a mixture of gases mainly nitrogen and oxygen, with small amounts of carbon dioxide, water vapour and other gases and dust.
examples: About four‑fifths of air is nitrogen and about one‑fifth is oxygen (concept level, not exact numbers needed).

concepts.title: Importance of Air
concepts.definition: Oxygen in air is essential for respiration; carbon dioxide is needed for photosynthesis; air supports burning and helps in formation of clouds and rain.
examples: A candle covered by a glass jar goes out when oxygen is used up.
Plants take in carbon dioxide and release oxygen during photosynthesis.

concepts.title: Air in Soil and Water
concepts.definition: Air is present in spaces between soil particles and dissolved in water, supplying oxygen to organisms living there.
examples: Digging or flooding soil releases air bubbles; shaking water leads to tiny bubbles as dissolved air comes out.

concepts.title: Types of Waste
concepts.definition: Waste includes all unwanted materials. It can be biodegradable (can be broken down by microorganisms) or non‑biodegradable (cannot easily be broken down).
examples: Vegetable peels and paper are biodegradable; plastics and metals are non‑biodegradable.

concepts.title: Waste Management and Recycling
concepts.definition: Proper collection, segregation, disposal and recycling of waste reduce pollution and health problems.
examples: Separating dry and wet waste in different bins; recycling paper and plastic; composting kitchen scraps.

concepts.title: Vermin Composting and Landfills
concepts.definition: Vermicomposting uses earthworms to turn organic waste into rich compost; landfills are sites for burying large amounts of non‑recyclable waste.
examples: A pit at home can be used for composting leaves with earthworms.
City garbage is often dumped in landfills outside towns.

concepts.title: Reduce, Reuse and Recycle
concepts.definition: The 3R principle encourages reducing waste generation, reusing items and recycling materials to conserve resources and protect environment.
examples: Carrying cloth bags instead of plastic, reusing bottles, and recycling newspapers follow the 3Rs.
